metadata.title: The Access Ladder: Scope Is a Security Decision

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The line that reframed everything is that read-only feels safe but the real risk is scope — a connected personnel file becomes source material that can surface in a Slack draft. I want to audit my own setup against the seven-rung ladder. Help me: (1) list every connector and folder Claude can currently reach and mark which rung each sits on; (2) for each, determine whether scope is the full account or a limitable folder; (3) identify which tasks only need three files and could drop to the uploaded-task-files rung instead.

props.command: The line that reframed everything is that read-only feels safe but the real risk is scope — a connected personnel file becomes source material that can surface in a Slack draft. I want to audit my own setup against the seven-rung ladder. Help me: (1) list every connector and folder Claude can currently reach and mark which rung each sits on; (2) for each, determine whether scope is the full account or a limitable folder; (3) identify which tasks only need three files and could drop to the uploaded-task-files rung instead.
props.segment: The Access Ladder: Scope Is a

beats.narration_text: A cloud connector gives Claude access to a service. It often gives Claude access to the entire service — not the folder you had in mind. Read permissions sound like a safe grant. They are not. If Claude can read a document, it can incorporate that document into any output it produces. The personnel file inside the connected scope is now source material. This is Bonjour, Liam — in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, connect Drive connector for project work

props.command: connect to my Drive and help me draft the project update

beats.narration_text: The access ladder has seven positions. Uploaded task files — you attach the specific files; access ends with the session. Local folder — a workspace you designate; scope risk is whatever is in the folder. Cloud connector — structured service access; scope is often the whole account. Browser — web page interaction; prompt-injection and logged-in-state risks. Computer use — screen interaction, highest supervision required. Plugins and MCP servers — bundled capability you may not have reviewed individually. Scheduled task — unattended action.
beats.new_visual_element: Seven-rung ladder — Upload / Folder / Connector / Browser / Computer use / Plugin / Scheduled

beats.narration_text: Read-only sounds safe. The risk is scope, not write access. Cowork can read anything inside the connected scope and draw on it for any output — summaries, recommendations, drafted messages. A sensitive document you forgot was in the connected folder can surface in a Slack draft or a report section. Scope control is the protection, not the permission level.
beats.new_visual_element: Read-not-safe diagram — read + wrong scope = sensitive doc in output
beats.spark_line: Read is not safe. Scope is.

beats.narration_text: Before enabling any connector, ask: what does this connector actually access? Is there a way to limit it to a specific folder or project, not the full account? If the connector exposes the full account and the task needs three files, use uploaded task files instead. The upload rung is not a limitation — it is the correct access scope for that task.
beats.new_visual_element: Connector audit question — full account vs. task files: which scope is right

beats.narration_text: The discipline: climb only as high as the task requires. A task done with uploaded files does not need a connector. A connector task does not need browser access. A browser task does not need computer use. Every step toward computer use also steps toward more brittleness and more prompt-injection exposure. Start at the bottom of the ladder. The lowest rung that completes the task is the right rung.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — lowest rung rule / climb only when below fails

beats.narration_text: Before your next connector session, audit the scope. Paste this.

props.command: Help me audit my Cowork access before I start. My task is: [describe it]. I was planning to use: [connector / folder / browser / upload]. Tell me: (1) what scope that access grants, (2) whether that scope is wider than the task requires, (3) the lowest ladder rung that can complete this task, (4) what I should restrict or exclude.

Every step toward computer use also steps toward more brittleness and more prompt-injection exposure. Start at the bottom of the ladder. The lowest rung that completes the task is the right rung.
